text: RAP Art

title: Defra Group RAP Champion Network
text: Contributors to this repository are expected to follow the Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct, and those working within Government are also expected to follow the Civil Service Code.

title: Defra Group RAP Champion Network
text:
Civil Service Code
The Civil Service Code

title: Defra Group RAP Champion Network
text:
Our Pledge
We as members, contributors, and leaders pledge to make participation in our community a harassment-free experience for everyone, regardless of age, body size, visible or invisible disability, ethnicity, sex characteristics, gender identity and expression, level of experience, education, socio-economic status, nationality, personal appearance, race, religion, or sexual identity and orientation.
We pledge to act and interact in ways that contribute to an open, welcoming, diverse, inclusive, and healthy community.

title: Defra Group RAP Champion Network
text:
Our Standards
Examples of behaviour that contributes to a positive environment for our community include:

Demonstrating empathy and kindness toward other people
Being respectful of differing opinions, viewpoints, and experiences
Giving and gracefully accepting constructive feedback
Accepting responsibility and apologizing to those affected by our mistakes, and learning from the experience
Focusing on what is best not just for us as individuals, but for the overall community

Examples of unacceptable behaviour include:

The use of sexualized language or imagery, and sexual attention or advances of any kind
Trolling, insulting or derogatory comments, and personal or political attacks
Public or private harassment
Publishing others’ private information, such as a physical or email address, without their explicit permission
Other conduct which could reasonably be considered inappropriate in a professional setting ## Enforcement Responsibilities Community leaders are responsible for clarifying and enforcing our standards of acceptable behaviour and will take appropriate and fair corrective action in response to any behaviour that they deem inappropriate, threatening, offensive, or harmful.

Community leaders have the right and responsibility to remove, edit, or reject comments, commits, code, wiki edits, issues, and other contributions that are not aligned to this Code of Conduct, and will communicate reasons for moderation decisions when appropriate.

title: Defra Group RAP Champion Network
text:
Scope
This Code of Conduct applies within all community spaces, and also applies when an individual is officially representing the community in public spaces. Examples of representing our community include using an official e-mail address, posting via an official social media account, or acting as an appointed representative at an online or offline event.

title: Defra Group RAP Champion Network
text:
Enforcement
Instances of abusive, harassing, or otherwise unacceptable behaviour may be reported to the community RAP champions. All complaints will be reviewed and investigated and will result in a response that is deemed necessary and appropriate to the circumstances. The project team is obligated to maintain confidentiality with regard to the reporter of an incident. Further details of specific enforcement policies may be posted separately.
Project maintainers who do not follow or enforce the Code of Conduct in good faith may face temporary or permanent repercussions as determined by other members of the project’s leadership.

title: Defra Group RAP Champion Network
text:
Attribution
This Code of Conduct is adapted from the Contributor Covenant, version 2.0 and the govcookiecutter Code of Conduct.
Community Impact Guidelines were inspired by Mozilla’s code of conduct enforcement ladder.
For answers to common questions about this code of conduct, see the FAQ. Translations are available here.

title: Maturity Levels of RAP
text:
The RAP Maturity Model is a reference for what good looks like when producing Reproducible Analytical Pipelines (RAPs). This guidance is the sum of learning and conversations with RAP champion networks across government from the Department for Education, NHS England, ONS and Analysis Function. Our maturity levels are defined as either ‘Good’, ‘Great’, or ‘Excellent’; these names have been chosen to make it clear that any steps taken to better align your project with RAP principles are beneficial and to recognise the level of RAP achieved can be proportional to your project. Each tier includes all the points from the previous tier, building on each to improve your analysis with RAP principles.
This guidance is set out by theme, within which, the minimum requirements for each maturity level of RAP against each theme are described. These themes are:

Automation
Quality Assurance & Testing
Reproducibility
Data Management

Each content theme has an option for ‘low-code’ RAPs; recognising that RAP principles can be met using software which requires minimal coding or none at all. Low-code software has become increasingly popular since the early 2020s, recognising this, this is the first Maturity Levels of RAP within the civil service to acknowledge this approach to create reproducible analytical pipelines. Low-code tools such as FME, MS Fabric, Power Query, Power BI however, are limited on their reproducibility as unlike open-source code software (R/Python/SQL etc), low-code software tends to be closed-source and therefore only reproducible if you purchase the software making true reproduction of analysis a limiting factor.
If you have any questions about this document which aren’t covered here, you can contact the Defra RAP Champions or send a message in the RAP Teams Channel.

title: Maturity Levels of RAP
text:
Theme: Automation


Table 1: Table: Automation {.striped .hover}






Level
Description




Low-code

Automate and minimise manual steps.
Manual steps must be documented in the README.
Pipeline runs on schedule or trigger.



Good

Automate and minimise manual steps.
Manual steps must be documented in the README.



Great

Outputs are automated with minimal intervention.
Pipeline runs on schedule or trigger.
Logs are automatically recorded.



Excellent

CI/CD runs tests (e.g. GitHub Actions, Databricks).
Test logs are recorded (pytest, testthat, etc.).

title: Maturity Levels of RAP
text:
Theme: Quality Assurance & Testing
Quality assurance is ubiquitous with analysis, and recommended by the Aqua book, you should strive to apply the most appropriate and proportionate quality assurance practices given the risks associated with your work.







Level
Low-code
Description

Pipeline has been peer reviewed by someone outside team this may involve dual running in another software to to check outputs are the same.





Good

Code and outputs have been peer reviewed within the team.



Great

More than one person has ownership of the code, e.g. through pair-coding.
Custom functions provide error messages.
Pipeline includes testing. We recommend unit tests to ensure functional “units” within your code operate as they should under different scenarios, like accommodating missing data, extreme values. Here, an will inform your unit tests.



Excellent

Code and outputs have been peer reviewed by someone not involved in development, e.g. outside of team.
Repository automatically runs tests via CI/CD

title: Maturity Levels of RAP
text:
Theme: Reproducibility
Reproducibility puts the R in RAP, if your RAP isn’t reproducible its an analytical pipeline. Reproducibility means your analysis is transparent and can be replicated, improving credibility of your results. Beyond this, reproducibility also preserves process knowledge management.
This is important in government, where analysis can be scrutinised through freedom of information requests or analytical pipeline results may be politically sensitive.
